exercises.title: Count the queries — spot the N+1
exercises.scenario: Identify how many SQL queries are fired for a given code block.
exercises.expected_solution:
Total queries with FetchType.LAZY:
  1 query: SELECT id, seller_id, name FROM products
  5 queries: SELECT * FROM sellers WHERE id=? (one per product)
  = 6 total (N+1 where N=5)

With FetchType.EAGER:
  Same or worse: Hibernate still fires SELECT * FROM sellers WHERE id=?
  for each product row (unless using JOIN)
  = Still N+1 (EAGER doesn't automatically use JOIN FETCH)

N+1 formula: 1 + N queries
  1 = main entity query
  N = one query per entity for each lazy association accessed

test_cases.expected:
Total queries with FetchType.LAZY:
  1 query: SELECT id, seller_id, name FROM products
  5 queries: SELECT * FROM sellers WHERE id=? (one per product)
  = 6 total (N+1 where N=5)

With FetchType.EAGER:
  Same or worse: Hibernate still fires SELECT * FROM sellers WHERE id=?
  for each product row (unless using JOIN)
  = Still N+1 (EAGER doesn't automatically use JOIN FETCH)

N+1 formula: 1 + N queries
  1 = main entity query
  N = one query per entity for each lazy association accessed

hints: LAZY: association loaded on first access (in the loop)
Each .getSeller() call can trigger a query if seller is not cached
FetchType.EAGER doesn't prevent N+1 — it just makes it fire earlier

exercises.title: Complete the JOIN FETCH query
exercises.scenario: JOIN FETCH resolves N+1 by loading association in the main query.
exercises.instructions: Fill in the JPQL to load orders with their user in a single query.
exercises.starter_code:
@Query("_______ o FROM Order o _____ _______ o.user WHERE o.status = :status")
List<Order> findByStatusWithUser(@Param("status") String status);
exercises.expected_solution:
@Query("SELECT o FROM Order o JOIN FETCH o.user WHERE o.status = :status")
List<Order> findByStatusWithUser(@Param("status") String status);

blanks.hint: Keyword that loads the association into memory

hints: JOIN FETCH: the two keywords that make Hibernate eagerly load the association
o.user: the association field name on the Order entity
Result: SELECT o.*, u.* FROM orders o INNER JOIN users u ON o.user_id=u.id WHERE o.status=?

exercises.title: Enable SQL logging to detect N+1
exercises.scenario: application.properties settings to see every SQL query in logs.
exercises.instructions:
Write the application.properties settings that:
1. Show all SQL queries in logs
2. Format SQL nicely (multi-line)
3. Show parameter values bound to queries
4. Enable Hibernate statistics
exercises.expected_solution:
# application.properties:
# Show SQL statements:
spring.jpa.show-sql=true

# Format multi-line (easier to read):
spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.format_sql=true

# Show bound parameter values:
logging.level.org.hibernate.type.descriptor.sql=trace
# Or for Hibernate 6+:
logging.level.org.hibernate.orm.jdbc.bind=trace

# Enable statistics (for programmatic query counting):
spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.generate_statistics=true
logging.level.org.hibernate.stat=debug
hints: spring.jpa.show-sql=true: bare minimum
format_sql=true: indents SQL for readability
generate_statistics=true: allows programmatic query counting

exercises.title: Write the N+1 causing code and identify it
exercises.scenario: Recognizing N+1 is the first step to fixing it.
exercises.instructions:
Given these entities:
- Trade (id, symbol, qty, price) with @ManyToOne User (name, email)
- User has many trades

Write the N+1 causing code in TradeService.getAllTradeSummaries() that:
1. Loads all trades
2. Maps each to TradeSummaryDto(symbol, qty, price, userName)
3. Mark every line that causes N+1 with a comment
4. State how many queries fire for 10 trades
hints: The N+1 happens inside the .map() — each trade.getUser() fires a query
1 query for findAll() + N queries for .getUser() = N+1 total
Mark with comments: // ← N+1: fires SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=?

exercises.title: Fix N+1 with JOIN FETCH
exercises.scenario: Rewrite the repository to eliminate N+1 with JOIN FETCH.
exercises.instructions:
Fix the N+1 from Ex 4 by adding a JOIN FETCH query to TradeRepository:
1. Add findAllWithUser() using @Query + JOIN FETCH
2. Update TradeService to use the new method
3. Show the SQL that Hibernate generates
hints: @Query("SELECT t FROM Trade t JOIN FETCH t.user"): loads t and user together
JOIN FETCH produces INNER JOIN in SQL
After JOIN FETCH: t.getUser() hits L1 cache, no extra query

exercises.title: Fix N+1 with @EntityGraph
exercises.scenario: @EntityGraph is an alternative to JOIN FETCH — no JPQL required.
exercises.instructions:
Fix N+1 in ProductRepository using @EntityGraph instead of JOIN FETCH:
1. Product has @ManyToOne Seller
2. Add @EntityGraph on findAll() to eagerly load seller
3. Add @EntityGraph on findByCategory() to eagerly load seller
4. Explain: when would you choose @EntityGraph over JOIN FETCH?
hints: @EntityGraph(attributePaths={"seller"}): loads seller with LEFT JOIN
Works on any method — derived queries, findAll, custom methods
LEFT JOIN: null seller allowed; INNER JOIN (JOIN FETCH): seller must exist

exercises.title: Hibernate statistics query count assertion
exercises.scenario: Test-driven verification that exactly 1 query fires (no N+1).
exercises.instructions:
Write a @DataJpaTest that:
1. Seeds 3 orders with their users using TestEntityManager
2. Calls testEm.clear() to evict L1 cache
3. Loads orders via findAllWithUser() (JOIN FETCH version)
4. Accesses user.getName() for each order
5. Asserts query count == 1 using Hibernate statistics
hints: testEm.clear(): MUST be called after seeding — evicts L1 cache
sf.getStatistics().clear(): reset count before the test query
getPreparedStatementCount(): counts actual SQL statements sent to DB

exercises.title: @OneToMany N+1 — restaurant with orders
exercises.scenario: @OneToMany relationships also suffer N+1 when traversed.
exercises.instructions:
Restaurant has @OneToMany List<Order> orders (lazy).
Write:
1. The N+1 causing code: load all restaurants and print order count
2. Fix using JOIN FETCH with DISTINCT
3. Alternative: @BatchSize(size=20) on the collection
4. Explain which fix to prefer and when
exercises.starter_code:
// @Entity Restaurant with @OneToMany List<Order> orders
// Show N+1 cause, JOIN FETCH DISTINCT fix, and @BatchSize alternative
hints: JOIN FETCH on @OneToMany: DISTINCT needed to prevent duplicate parent entities
@BatchSize: Hibernate batches N loads into IN (...) queries
JOIN FETCH + Pageable (OneToMany): HHH90003004 warning → in-memory pagination

exercises.title: Multiple associations — choosing what to JOIN FETCH
exercises.scenario: Not all associations need JOIN FETCH — only those actually used.
exercises.instructions:
Order has @ManyToOne User, @ManyToOne Restaurant, @OneToMany List<OrderItem>.

Write 3 specific repository methods:
1. findAllForDashboard(): needs user name and restaurant name (not items)
2. findByIdWithItems(id): needs all order items (for order detail page)
3. findByUserIdSummary(userId): needs only user and status (for history page)

Each should JOIN FETCH only what it needs — minimize data transfer.
hints: Only JOIN FETCH what the caller actually accesses in the response
JOIN FETCH unused associations wastes memory and bandwidth
DISTINCT for @OneToMany JOIN FETCH: avoids duplicate parent objects

exercises.title: L1 cache and query count — tricky scenario
exercises.scenario: The first-level cache can hide N+1 in tests.
exercises.expected_solution:
count may print 1 (or fewer than expected N+1)

Reason: L1 cache (Session cache) masking the N+1.

When testEm.persistAndFlush(new Trade("TCS", u)) is called,
Hibernate stores User 'Aisha' in the current Session's L1 cache.

When repo.findAll() loads the trades (1 query),
and then t.getUser().getName() is called:
→ Hibernate checks L1 cache FIRST
→ Finds User 'Aisha' already cached
→ Returns from cache — NO additional SQL query!

So query count = 1 (just findAll), even though no JOIN FETCH.
This HIDES the N+1 bug.

Fix: call testEm.clear() BEFORE the action being tested.
clear() evicts the L1 cache → getUser() must hit DB → N+1 becomes visible.

test_cases.expected:
count may print 1 (or fewer than expected N+1)

Reason: L1 cache (Session cache) masking the N+1.

When testEm.persistAndFlush(new Trade("TCS", u)) is called,
Hibernate stores User 'Aisha' in the current Session's L1 cache.

When repo.findAll() loads the trades (1 query),
and then t.getUser().getName() is called:
→ Hibernate checks L1 cache FIRST
→ Finds User 'Aisha' already cached
→ Returns from cache — NO additional SQL query!

So query count = 1 (just findAll), even though no JOIN FETCH.
This HIDES the N+1 bug.

Fix: call testEm.clear() BEFORE the action being tested.
clear() evicts the L1 cache → getUser() must hit DB → N+1 becomes visible.

hints: L1 cache stores entities per Hibernate Session — persisted entities are cached
clear() evicts all entities — forces DB hit on next access
Without clear(), your 'fix' test may pass for the wrong reason

exercises.title: Named @EntityGraph on @Entity class
exercises.scenario: @NamedEntityGraph defines reusable fetch plans at the entity level.
exercises.instructions:
Define a @NamedEntityGraph on the Order @Entity, then use it in the repository:
1. @NamedEntityGraph named 'Order.withUserAndRestaurant' loading user + restaurant
2. Second graph 'Order.withItems' loading items (OneToMany)
3. Repository uses named graphs on findByStatus() and findById()
4. Explain benefit of named graphs vs @EntityGraph inline
exercises.starter_code:
// @Entity Order with @NamedEntityGraph(s)
// OrderRepository using named graphs
hints: @NamedEntityGraphs: multiple graphs on one entity
@NamedAttributeNode: lists the association paths to eagerly load
Repository uses the named graph by string name in @EntityGraph("name")

exercises.title: DTO projection — avoid loading entities entirely
exercises.scenario: For read-only responses, load only what you need into a DTO.
exercises.instructions:
Write a DTO projection for the order listing page:
1. Interface-based projection: OrderListView with getSymbol(), getQty(), getUserName()
2. @Query returning the projection with nested field access (user.name)
3. Class-based DTO: OrderSummaryDto record with constructor
4. Compare: projection vs JOIN FETCH — when to use each
hints: Interface projection: Spring generates SQL for only the fields in the interface
@Value("#{target.user.name}"): SpEL for nested field in interface projection
Class DTO JPQL: new fully.qualified.ClassName(field1, field2, ...)

exercises.title: Complete N+1 fix + test for Swiggy order service
exercises.scenario: End-to-end: identify N+1, fix, test with query count assertion.
exercises.instructions:
Build a Swiggy order service:
1. Order @Entity: user @ManyToOne, restaurant @ManyToOne, status, total
2. OrderRepository: N+1 causing findAll() AND fixed findAllWithDetails()
3. OrderService: getAllOrderDtos() uses fixed method
4. @DataJpaTest (3 tests): seed 5 orders, verify query count=1, verify 5 results, verify user name correct

All 3 tests pass.
exercises.starter_code:
// Swiggy order service with N+1 fix
// Order @Entity, OrderRepository, OrderService, OrderServiceTest (@DataJpaTest)
hints: testEm.clear() after seeding: mandatory for honest query count testing
@Import(SwiggyOrderService.class): makes service available in @DataJpaTest
JOIN FETCH both user AND restaurant: else 2N+1 queries

exercises.title: JOIN FETCH vs pagination — the in-memory trap
exercises.scenario: JOIN FETCH + Pageable on @OneToMany causes Hibernate to paginate in memory.
exercises.instructions:
Write code that demonstrates the problem and the fix:
1. Show: Restaurant findAll(Pageable) with @OneToMany orders JOIN FETCH → HHH90003004 warning
2. Show: correct fix using @BatchSize(size=20) on the orders collection
3. Show: alternative fix using 2-step approach (IDs first, then IN query)
4. Write a comparison method comment explaining which to use when
exercises.starter_code:
// Restaurant @Entity with @OneToMany orders
// Show the JOIN FETCH + Pageable problem and 3 fix approaches
hints: @BatchSize: replaces N SELECT WHERE id=? with SELECT WHERE id IN (...)
2-step approach: paginate on IDs (scalable), then fetch full with IN query
JOIN FETCH + @OneToMany + Pageable = always wrong (HHH90003004)

exercises.title: Complete Zerodha portfolio service — N+1 synthesis
exercises.scenario: Full service with multiple association types, each fixed correctly.
exercises.instructions:
Build Zerodha portfolio service with N+1 fixes:
1. Trade @Entity: user @ManyToOne, @ManyToOne Instrument (symbol, fullName, sector)
2. User @Entity: @OneToMany List<Trade> trades (lazy, @BatchSize=30)
3. TradeRepository: 3 methods — all with @EntityGraph/JOIN FETCH as appropriate
4. PortfolioService: getTradeSummaries(userId) + getInstrumentStats(symbol)
5. @DataJpaTest (3 tests): query count=1 for getTradeSummaries, correct results

All 3 tests pass.
hints: @BatchSize on @OneToMany: batches lazy loads into IN queries
JOIN FETCH both user AND instrument: 1 query loads all 3 tables
testEm.clear() mandatory before query count assertion
